title: Building Faster Cryptography with Carryless Multiplication in NVIDIA CUDA 13.3 | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA CUDA 13.3 adds clmad, a hardware-accelerated carryless multiply-accumulate PTX instruction available on all Ampere-and-newer GPUs (SM 80+), closing a 15-year gap versus x86's PCLMULQDQ; it delivers up to 18.8x faster GHASH (reaching ~6.3 TB/s on B200, near DRAM bandwidth) for AES-GCM authenticated encryption and 3-13x faster sum-check protocol performance for GF(2^128) zero-knowledge proofs, retroactively enabled on already-deployed GPUs.

insights.claim: x86 CPUs have had a dedicated hardware carryless-multiplication instruction (PCLMULQDQ) for over fifteen years, but NVIDIA GPUs lacked native support until CUDA 13.3 introduced clmad, retroactively enabled in hardware on all Ampere-and-newer GPUs (SM 80+) already deployed.
insights.speaker: Jean-Luc Watson
insights.so_what: Because clmad activates existing hardware capability on already-shipped Ampere-or-later GPUs via a software/driver update, the performance gains are effectively free capacity unlock for the installed base — a meaningful software-driven value-add NVIDIA can point to without new hardware sales.
insights.quote: For over fifteen years, x86 CPUs have shipped with a dedicated hardware instruction for carryless multiplication.

insights.claim: On NVIDIA B200, GHASH (the integrity hash inside AES-GCM, the AEAD cipher behind TLS, VPN and most data-center encryption) reaches ~6.3 TB/s throughput using clmad — close to DRAM read bandwidth and up to 18.8x faster than the prior bitsliced state of the art.
insights.speaker: Jean-Luc Watson
insights.so_what: GHASH throughput approaching DRAM bandwidth means encryption is no longer the bottleneck for data-center TLS/VPN traffic processed on GPU — relevant for hyperscalers offloading network encryption workloads onto GPU fleets instead of dedicated crypto ASICs.
insights.quote: we observe a peak throughput of ~6,335 GB/s (close to the DRAM read bandwidth), which exhibits up to 18.8x higher throughput than the bitsliced variant

insights.claim: On the bitsliced (pre-clmad) baseline, the B200 was actually slower than the GeForce RTX 5090 for GHASH due to fewer SMs and lower clock speed, but clmad's hardware acceleration flips this so the B200 becomes dramatically faster — illustrating that new instruction-level hardware support can outweigh raw core-count/clock differences.
insights.speaker: Jean-Luc Watson
insights.so_what: A reminder that GPU generational comparisons based on core count or clock speed alone can be misleading once workload-specific hardware instructions (like clmad) are introduced — investors evaluating GPU competitive positioning should weight ISA-level features, not just raw specs.
insights.quote: On the bitsliced baseline, the B200 is actually slower than the RTX 5090 (fewer SMs, lower clock), but CLMAD’s hardware acceleration makes its GHASH very fast.

insights.claim: The sum-check protocol — the core primitive underlying zero-knowledge proving systems such as Binius that operate over GF(2^128) — is accelerated 3-4x on RTX 5090 and up to 13x on B200 using clmad versus the prior bitsliced-circuit state of the art from Irreducible.
insights.speaker: Jean-Luc Watson
insights.so_what: A 13x speedup in ZK sum-check on already-deployed GPUs materially lowers the cost of generating zero-knowledge proofs, which is directly relevant to blockchain/crypto infrastructure (ZK-rollups, privacy protocols) that are proof-generation-bottlenecked — a potential cost-structure shift for ZK-based crypto projects using GPU proving.

insights.claim: The authors argue carryless multiplication is a shared kernel underneath a wide range of cryptographic and coding-theoretic workloads beyond AES-GCM — including CRC and Reed-Solomon codes (storage, telecom baseband), BCH codes (flash memory), quantum stabilizer codes, several post-quantum cryptographic schemes, and binary-field zero-knowledge proving systems — meaning clmad changes the cost structure for all of them simultaneously on every Ampere-or-later GPU already deployed.
insights.speaker: Jean-Luc Watson
insights.so_what: A single ISA-level feature unlocking broad cost reductions across storage, telecom, flash memory, and post-quantum crypto workloads suggests downstream productivity/cost effects across multiple infrastructure verticals, not just one narrow crypto niche — worth tracking for GPU-based offload adoption in storage and telecom (e.g., NVIDIA Aerial).
insights.locator: Why does this matter beyond AES-GCM
